posts.summary: The post argues that audience-based companies like Facebook, Twitter, and Snapchat function essentially as public goods but are forced into traditional for-profit models that require monetization through advertising and data collection. Using Wikipedia as a counterexample, Asparouhova suggests these platforms could have remained neutral communication utilities as nonprofits rather than pursuing IPOs. She examines emerging funding mechanisms—syndicates, crowdfunding, and nonprofit-friendly investment vehicles—that could enable valuable tech innovations to prioritize impact over billion-dollar exits. The piece concludes that funders hold the power to reshape incentives and create alternatives to the current system.
key_points: Audience companies like Facebook are essentially public goods (nonexcludable, nonrivalrous) that we've awkwardly forced into for-profit business models
Wikipedia chose nonprofit status to preserve neutrality—Facebook could have made the same choice but didn't, leading to data monetization
The current funding ecosystem only offers two exits (acquisition or IPO), constraining how valuable services can operate
New funding vehicles (AngelList Syndicates, crowdfunding platforms, nonprofit-friendly funds) are emerging that could support impact-focused companies
The responsibility for change lies with funders who write the checks, not startups chasing unicorn valuations
money_quotes: Having Facebook on a mobile phone is more important than being able to browse anything else on the web or use any other mobile app. It's a communication lifeline.
What if tech were valued in our society because it helps us build useful things for the world on the cheap, not because of its inflated valuations and perceived glamorous lifestyle?
In the offline world, not everything that provides value is a for-profit corporation. The online world, as it matures, will eventually follow suit.
It would be naive to assume that startups will learn to do the 'right thing' and not pursue their dreams of mega-billion unicorns. They're currently not being incentivized to do otherwise.
It's up to funders—those who are writing the checks—to take a step back and think about what kind of world they want to create.

This type of company didn't exist before the Internet, and arguably, not before 2002, with the launch of Friendster. We didn't have a set of rules for them yet, so we backdated them into our existing business rules.
posts.key_insight: The structure of tech funding—not the nature of technology itself—forces valuable communication platforms into advertising-driven business models that compromise user privacy and neutrality.

posts.summary: Nadia argues that Silicon Valley's casual aesthetic masks a new form of materialism centered on services rather than possessions. While tech workers reject traditional status symbols like luxury cars and designer goods, they pour money into on-demand services that maximize their time. This 'service economy' creates an ambiguous class dynamic where service providers and customers may be demographically identical. The essay concludes that despite anti-materialist rhetoric, tech workers are just as dependent on their purchased conveniences as traditional wealthy elites were on their possessions.
key_points: Silicon Valley materialism manifests through services (Uber, TaskRabbit, laundry apps) rather than physical possessions
Time is the ultimate status symbol in tech culture—wealth is measured by what you can hire others to do
The gig economy blurs class distinctions when Lyft drivers and passengers share the same demographics
The 'anti-capitalism capitalism' of Silicon Valley obscures traditional employer-employee power dynamics
Tech workers remain just as dependent on purchased conveniences as traditional elites were on physical luxuries
money_quotes: Silicon Valley types are characterized as people who care about not caring.
Your wealth isn't measured by what you own, but rather what you can hire other people to do for you.
Time is the most valuable thing in Silicon Valley. And the wealthiest can afford to capture as much of that time as possible.
Are you a Lyft driver, or are you someone who takes Lyfts?
It's just not as obvious when the person you're hiring might be someone you meet at a party later that night.
Even if we came here for the impact, we're staying for the money - and benefiting from it no differently than a Wall Street banker.
posts.key_insight: Silicon Valley hasn't rejected materialism—it has simply redirected conspicuous consumption from objects to services, with time rather than possessions as the ultimate status symbol.

posts.summary: Asparouhova argues that 'doing good' shouldn't be confined to traditional nonprofit structures or explicit social causes like B Corps. She contends that innovation and positive social impact can come from any sector, pointing to companies like Facebook, Airbnb, and Lyft as examples of for-profits creating societal value. The post challenges readers to evaluate companies based on their actual impact and motivations rather than their tax status or labels. She suggests that tech companies without clear revenue models, like Snapchat, might benefit from exploring alternative funding structures similar to those in the social sector.
key_points: B Corps and nonprofit labels risk alienating rather than normalizing the idea that companies doing good come in all shapes and sizes
Larry Page's preference to leave money to Elon Musk over charity reflects a lack of faith in the social sector's ability to innovate, not a lack of interest in doing good
Tax status should be determined by the economics of a model, not the cause being tackled
Tech companies without clear revenue models should consider alternative funding structures beyond maximizing profit
The social sector is beginning to shift focus from low-risk handouts to tackling bigger, riskier systemic problems
money_quotes: Doing good can also mean things like connecting people with each other (Facebook), creating a more empathetic world (Secret), helping people trust each other (Craigslist), or enabling more people to earn income on their own terms (AirBnB).
Labels like these run the risk of alienating rather than normalizing the idea that companies doing good come in all shapes and sizes.
I truly hope that the term 'nonprofit' becomes associated with big risks and building better systems rather than charity on a shoestring.
Companies become nonprofits when being profit-driven might compromise the value and integrity of the service they provide.
Your tax status isn't determined by the cause you tackle. It's based on the economics of your model.
Are they preserving the status quo, or solving big, hairy problems? Are they excited to make an impact on their society, or are they in it for recognition or money?
What should we do with tech companies like Snapchat who are clearly innovating and providing value, but don't have a clear revenue model?
posts.key_insight: We should evaluate companies by their actual impact and motivations rather than by labels like nonprofit, B Corp, or for-profit—doing good isn't limited by tax status or sector.

posts.summary: Asparouhova draws parallels between philanthropic 'innovation funding' and venture capital, arguing both pursue high-risk, high-return strategies but in different domains—social vs. financial returns. She identifies lessons each sector can learn from the other: philanthropists should embrace portfolio thinking where not every investment needs to succeed, while VCs should recognize the externalities and social value inherent in their work. The post advocates for cross-pollination of ideas between sectors that are structurally similar despite their different objectives.
key_points: Innovation funding and venture capital are structurally identical strategies—both pursue high-risk, high-return opportunities—differing only in whether returns are social or financial
Philanthropic funders have become too risk-averse, expecting every investment to show evidence of success, which leads to safer but less transformative grants
VCs should recognize that their portfolio companies create externalities and social value beyond pure financial returns
Both sectors would benefit from embracing a 'double bottom line' that acknowledges financial and social returns together
The ecosystems created to effect change are fundamentally similar regardless of sector
money_quotes: Innovation funding and venture capital differ only in that the former is aimed at social returns, the latter on financial returns.
In the social sector, funders expect that each of their investments can show evidence of impact. This expectation leads to investing in lower-risk, but smaller opportunities.
The nature of the beast is to swing for the fences.
Funders in the social sector recognize that change happens in an ecosystem, not a silo.
Creating and nurturing the next big thing in the world is exciting, and a big reason why VCs are in the business.
The parallels that can be drawn between innovation funding and venture capital serve as a reminder that the ecosystems we create to effect change are similar, no matter the sector they tackle.
posts.key_insight: Philanthropic innovation funding and venture capital are mirror images of the same investment philosophy, and practitioners in both sectors would benefit from adopting each other's frameworks for risk tolerance, timeline expectations, and measuring success.

posts.summary: Asparouhova argues that major tech corporations like Google and Facebook have become de-facto governments of the internet, providing essential 'public services' that form the backbone of online communication. She notes that while governments provide offline infrastructure like streetlights and waste management, corporations now provide the equivalent online utilities. However, unlike governments, these corporations are legally obligated to serve shareholders, not the public. She questions whether this model is sustainable, especially as ephemeral and anonymous apps emerge that don't fit traditional advertising models.
key_points: Internet services like search, email, and social media function as public utilities but are controlled by a handful of corporations
High-value acquisitions can be reframed as corporations 'sponsoring' services to continue serving society under centralized management
For-profit corporations cannot be neutral governing bodies because their legal obligation is to shareholders, not citizens
Ephemeral and anonymous apps represent a challenge to the advertising-based model that funds internet infrastructure
The question of how to sustainably incubate essential internet services remains unresolved
money_quotes: If government's role is, at least in part, to support the functional backbone services of our society, these corporations have become our de-facto governments.
Companies like Google and Facebook might serve the same purpose online as government entities offline, but they are not - cannot be - a neutral entity. Even if they wanted to be.
It shouldn't ever be a for-profit corporation's job to serve the role of public government.
They sold to a corporation, who would sponsor their service so that it could continue to serve society while being managed by a centralized power.
The next wave of Internet communication - ephemeral and anonymous apps - do not collect data on their users. The old advertising models don't work as easily on these products.
posts.key_insight: Tech corporations have assumed governmental roles in providing essential internet infrastructure, but their profit obligations make them fundamentally unsuited to serve as neutral public institutions.

posts.summary: The post critiques the use of historical data to predict future outcomes, using Google Flu Trends as a cautionary example. GFT's algorithm failed because it depended on Google Search behavior remaining constant, which it didn't. The author extends this lesson to startup investing, arguing that the rapidly evolving tech landscape makes historical patterns unreliable predictors of success. She notes that metrics, founder archetypes, and success criteria all change faster than our models can adapt. The lack of accountability for failed predictions compounds the problem, as failures are quickly forgotten while luck explains away outliers.
key_points: Google Flu Trends failed because it used a static algorithm while the underlying measurement tool (Google Search) was constantly changing
Startup success patterns are particularly volatile—what worked in 2005 doesn't predict 2014, and metrics like Twitter followers or zero-revenue valuations didn't exist before
The 'next big thing' is almost by definition something we aren't prepared for, making historical pattern-matching inherently flawed
Luck serves as an accountability escape hatch—outlier successes are attributed to luck, and failures are forgotten within months
As industries evolve rapidly, data-driven predictions require humility about the shifting ground beneath our models
money_quotes: Using the same algorithm from 2008 to make predictions about flu outbreaks depended upon Google search algorithms and user search behavior being roughly the same as they were in 2008, which they weren't.
We don't know what will make the next startup successful based on what's worked in the past, because the standards for success change so rapidly.
Almost by definition, the 'next big thing' is going to be something we aren't prepared for.
We don't know what the best metrics are to measure the next generation of startups, because we don't yet know how they will be measured.
Revenue probably once sounded like a reliable metric 20 years ago, but now, it's possible for a company to have zero revenue and be valued at over $1B.
If a startup defies the algorithm, we just say they got lucky.
The ground is moving beneath our feet even as we try to build upon it.
posts.key_insight: Predictive models built on historical data become unreliable when the underlying systems they measure are themselves rapidly evolving.

posts.summary: Asparouhova argues that while cultural critics have focused on technology addiction, the next frontier of satire will be our relationship with internet empathy. She uses examples like Kony 2012 and viral poverty essays to show how people engage emotionally with online content regardless of its authenticity. The core tension she identifies is whether internet-mediated empathy is genuine connection or 'masturbatory'—a controlled, selfish pursuit of emotional stimulation. She suggests the internet lets us curate our outrage and joy, potentially making empathy a self-serving act rather than true concern for others.
key_points: Cultural critiques lag behind reality—screen addiction satire is already dated while new problems emerge
People often don't care if emotionally compelling internet content is fake, as long as it 'feels good'
Internet empathy may come at the cost of empathy for the physical world around us
The internet allows unprecedented control over our emotional experiences, letting us curate outrage and joy
The line between altruism and selfishness in digital engagement is increasingly blurred
money_quotes: However, as we become more empathetic to what's on our screens, we risk becoming less empathetic to the physical world around us.

It begs the question whether Internet empathy is simply masturbatory: a reflexive search to relieve our desires for joy and anger and emotion through external stimuli that could just as easily be fake as it is real.
But maybe we don't care, because we want to believe that it all matters.
The internet allows us to control our empathy.
Fiction or non-fiction, it felt good then and it feels good now.
The empathy that the internet has created may be purely selfish, after all.
The internet has amplified that, made it harder to know when we're caring about others or just caring about ourselves.
posts.key_insight: The internet hasn't made us more empathetic—it's given us unprecedented control over our empathy, turning genuine human connection into curated emotional self-service.

posts.summary: The post challenges the notion that technological progress is unstoppable by arguing that humanity has repeatedly chosen to limit or abandon innovations when they threaten our wellbeing. Using chemical warfare and processed food as historical examples, Asparouhova suggests that society eventually 'recalibrates' after recognizing the downsides of new technologies. She draws a parallel to today's big data and surveillance era, suggesting we may still be in an 'infatuation phase' with personalization and data collection, not yet ready to confront its pitfalls.
key_points: Progress is inevitable, but advancing technology is not humanity's sole goal - survival requires balancing innovation against moral and physical limitations
The 'chemical taboo' demonstrates how humanity collectively chose to abandon a destructive technology despite having the capability
Post-WWII food processing enthusiasm eventually gave way to slow food and local produce movements, showing we value more than pure technological efficiency
We may be in an 'infatuation phase' with big data and personalization, similar to earlier eras' excitement about processed food
Technological innovation without context is meaningless - history shows we often need decades of mistakes before recalibrating
money_quotes: The sole goal of humanity is to survive, which means that technological innovation must be weighed against consideration of our moral and physical limitations.
Only the former keeps our numbers growing; only the latter keeps us from destroying ourselves too quickly.
We had the technology to mutilate our enemies, but we decided not to.
We had the technology to do away with food, but we decided not to.
Maybe we're still in the infatuation phase, basking in the glow of a screen that knows exactly who we are and what we want, food scientists gazing blissfully upon our glorious, jiggling Jell-O molds.
Technological innovation for its own sake is meaningless without context.
posts.key_insight: History shows that humanity repeatedly embraces then retreats from technologies that threaten our wellbeing - and we may be due for a similar reckoning with big data and algorithmic personalization.

posts.summary: This data analysis examines Product Hunt's contributor base and product categories by manually tagging 1,023 products. The author found that contributor reputation (both posting frequency and Twitter followers) doesn't correlate with product success—it's the product itself that matters. The analysis reveals that Developer Tools, Design, and Product Updates dominate, reflecting the hacker/designer nature of the community. Notably, 91% of contributors are male. The key insight is that Product Hunt differs from startup databases because it includes fun, quirky projects that were never meant to be venture-backed companies—it's fundamentally a place for product lovers, not just startup scouts.
key_points: A contributor's reputation (posting frequency or Twitter followers) has no correlation with how well a product performs on Product Hunt—success depends on the product itself
91% of Product Hunt contributors are male, only 9% female, revealing significant gender disparity in tech product curation
The top categories (Developer Tools, Design, Product Updates) reflect the hacker/designer nature of the community, with people posting things useful to themselves
Product Hunt serves a dual function: discovering new products AND tracking updates to existing products (Product Updates ranked #3)
Unlike AngelList or Crunchbase, many Product Hunt submissions aren't meant to be startups—they're fun, quirky projects that just make people smile
money_quotes: It's possible to post something just once that turns out to be popular. How well the product is received probably has more to do with the product itself.
I doubt the creators of @pmarca-tweet-essays or Emoji Zone are trying to raise a $1M seed round for their ideas. They're just fun, quirky, wonderful places on the internet.
Product Hunt overlaps with the world of startup databases and sourcing tools in a big way, but we shouldn't forget that at its core, it's a place for product lovers.
There is joy to be found in opening up Product Hunt and finding yourself unexpectedly wandering through the solar system or experiencing a light year.
The excitement of discovering quick-fix products that make life better is, I imagine, what keeps many Product Hunters coming back.
Picking just one category per product was more art than science.
posts.key_insight: Product Hunt is fundamentally a community for product lovers sharing useful and delightful discoveries, not just a startup sourcing tool—and a product's success depends entirely on the product itself, not who posts it.

posts.summary: Asparouhova argues that the controversy around Yo's $1M funding reveals a deeper cultural shift: entrepreneurs have become this generation's most influential artists. She contends that startups have evolved from purely technical or business ventures into creative expressions, with technology serving as the new canvas for imagination. Drawing parallels to the Dadaist movement and the Lost Generation of writers, she suggests that the public debate over startups mirrors historical reactions to avant-garde art. The piece concludes that while we obsess over tech valuations, we're overlooking that we're simultaneously making cultural history.
key_points: Startups have shifted from being about technical ability or business acumen to being about creative output
The lowered bar for software development has allowed creative minds to flock to tech as an expressive medium
The post-2008 recession appeal of entrepreneurship lies in creative freedom and societal influence while still being a 'real job'
Public debate over startups like Yo mirrors historical reactions to avant-garde art movements like Dadaism
Geographic location influences entrepreneurs similarly to how Paris influenced the Lost Generation writers
money_quotes: Somewhere between Google and Yo, startups, and our speculation around them, became less about technical ability or business acumen and more about creative output.
Collectively, we're captivated by the potential of technology, a place to let one's imagination run wild and envision the world as it should be rather than as it is.
It's as easy now to turn those ideas into code as it is to put a paintbrush to paper.

The exploding appeal of entrepreneurship in the wake of the 2008 recession lies in the relief: a place to think creatively, live freely, and influence society…not to mention a semblance of a real job to write home about.
We're so focused on making history for technology that we forget we're making history for culture, too.
Is Yo worth a $1M investment? I don't know. Was Marcel Duchamp's 'Fountain' worth $1.7M?
posts.key_insight: The cultural significance of startups extends far beyond their business value—they represent this generation's dominant form of creative expression and cultural commentary.

posts.summary: The post examines how peer-to-peer platforms like Udemy, Skillshare, Uber, and Lyft struggle to differentiate at scale because they cannot own their supply or demand—both providers and consumers freely move between competing platforms. Asparouhova argues this dynamic pushes P2P businesses toward becoming monopolies or remaining indistinguishable from competitors. She suggests these platforms function more like governments or conduits overseeing human behavior than traditional corporations. The post raises open questions about whether the optimal structure for P2P businesses is public corporation, private company, or nonprofit, and whether traditional investment models are appropriate for this new category.
key_points: P2P platforms cannot own their supply or demand—teachers, drivers, and customers freely use multiple competing services
At scale, P2P competitors become indistinguishable (Uber vs Lyft), suggesting these models must achieve monopoly status or fail to differentiate
P2P platforms function more like governments or conduits than traditional corporations
Competing with P2P monopolies requires dismantling them piece-by-piece rather than head-on competition
Traditional investment and financing models may not be appropriate for P2P businesses
money_quotes: No peer-to-peer online learning platform can own its supply or demand, which has made it difficult for a clear winner to emerge at scale.
It's great for those of us who participate on these platforms, because it means the company's value is concentrated in the people's, not the corporation's, hands.
It seems that successful pure peer-to-peer models have to be monopolies in their respective markets, or else they will be indistinguishable from their competitors at scale.
Rather than corporations, perhaps these peer-to-peer models are conduits, not dissimilar to governments, overseeing a certain class of human behavior.
To compete with a peer-to-peer monopoly, new companies entering the market would need to dismantle it piece-by-piece rather than compete with it head on.
posts.key_insight: Peer-to-peer platforms that cannot own their supply or demand must either achieve monopoly status or become indistinguishable from competitors, functioning more like governmental conduits than traditional corporations.

posts.summary: Asparouhova observes a pattern where companies fund experimental or hard-to-monetize consumer products through more lucrative B2B contracts or licensing deals. She draws parallels to Amazon's strategy of offering unprofitable features to capture market share. The post raises the question of whether this cross-subsidization model could enable businesses that are valuable to society but difficult to monetize ethically, like ad-free social networks or journalism. She notes the tension between Ello's ad-free manifesto and its venture funding, questioning how they'll produce returns without advertising.
key_points: Companies are using high-margin B2B businesses to fund experimental B2C products
Decoupling products from their profitability enables business models that avoid ethically problematic monetization like ads
Duolingo exemplifies this by funding free language learning through selling crowd-sourced translations
There's clear public demand for ad-free social networks (App.net, Diaspora, Ello) but unclear paths to sustainability
Acquisitions partially address this need but create their own conflicts of interest
money_quotes: Basically, use a reliable and lucrative high-margin business to fund an experimental, lower-margin part of their business.
What if a company used high-margin parts of their business to fund harder-to-ethically-monetize businesses like social networks, media companies, or journalism?
By setting up their business this way, they don't have to resort to ad sales like many other mobile apps do.
We can all agree that there is a desire to see an ad-free social network, given public interest in App.net, Diaspora, and now Ello.
I'm curious whether more companies use this business model to fund products that are valuable to society, but are hard to monetize.
posts.key_insight: Cross-subsidizing socially valuable but hard-to-monetize products with profitable business lines could be an alternative to ad-driven models that compromise user interests.

posts.summary: Asparouhova argues that while transparency offers significant benefits to organizations and society, privacy remains essential and should not be diminished in pursuit of openness. Writing in response to Buffer's radically transparent fundraising announcement and Dave Eggers' dystopian novel 'The Circle,' she makes the case that privacy enables creativity, moral development, intimacy, and authenticity. She contends that people need private spaces to experiment, fail, and develop their character without the inhibiting effects of observation. The post serves as a counterweight to the tech industry's transparency-as-virtue narrative.
key_points: Research shows people become creatively inhibited when observed—privacy enables the willingness to look silly that creativity requires
Privacy provides a safe space for moral experimentation, allowing people to learn consequences firsthand and build character
The contrast between private and public selves enables intimacy and authentic human connection
Constant observation causes people to perform for others rather than act authentically, risking society becoming 'an echo chamber of our public selves'
money_quotes: I truly believe that transparency can offer unlimited benefits to society, as long as it does not diminish the value of privacy.
Creativity requires not being afraid to look silly, and we don't want to look silly in front of other people.
Privacy gives us a safe space to conduct moral experiments.
We need to know what happens if we cheat and cover it up, or we tell a lie and get away with it.
So much of who we are as humans stems from the contrast between our private and public selves.
If everything were completely transparent, we could not fully appreciate intimate moments.
We act differently when we know we're being watched. By definition, that is the value of transparency.
Without privacy, we lose a sense of authenticity, and society risks becoming an echo chamber of our public selves.
posts.key_insight: Privacy is not merely the absence of transparency but a positive good that enables creativity, moral growth, intimacy, and authenticity—qualities that would be impossible in a fully transparent world.

posts.summary: Asparouhova observes that women in her peer group tend to pursue graduate school while men gravitate toward tech and entrepreneurship. She draws a parallel to Freakonomics' analysis of baby naming trends, suggesting that prestigious career paths may follow similar patterns—becoming attractive to men first, then 'trickling down' to women just as they lose their cachet. She notes that programming was once considered women's work until it gained status, at which point women were pushed out. The provocative conclusion: women may never have been collectively represented in positions considered powerful by their contemporaries, and true career strategy means staying ahead of trends rather than following them.
key_points: 58% of first-time graduate students are women, while men increasingly pursue tech and entrepreneurship
Career prestige may follow Freakonomics-style patterns: high-status groups adopt paths first, then abandon them as others follow
Programming was considered 'women's work' in the 1960s until it gained status and prestige, then women were pushed out
Women may succeed in becoming tech founders in 30 years, but only after the role loses its current prestige
The strategic career move is to anticipate the next prestigious path rather than following current trends
money_quotes: Maybe graduate school was the thing to do until recently, but now men have turned their eye toward pursuing careers in tech and entrepreneurship, leaving women behind.

Perhaps it's not that things were once better and then got worse. It's that women have simply never been collectively represented in a position considered to be respected and powerful by their contemporaries.

30 years from now, I can confidently predict there will be plenty of women who are tech founders, even if we cease all gender equality efforts right now. But did that mean we succeeded, if being a tech founder is no longer en vogue?
Rather than focus on getting a graduate degree, learning to code, or starting a company, the goal should be to stay ahead of the trends.
posts.key_insight: Women may consistently achieve representation in prestigious career paths only after those paths have lost their status, suggesting the real barrier isn't access but the moving target of what society values.

posts.summary: The post explores how single-LP venture capital models could enable more experimental and patient funding approaches. By examining examples like Kapor Capital, Omidyar Network, Bloomberg Beta, and Breakout Labs, Asparouhova argues that when investors don't need to satisfy multiple LPs demanding traditional returns, they gain flexibility to fund unconventional ventures. She suggests this represents an opportunity for tech wealth to return to philanthropy's original purpose: giving society freedom to take risks on innovations that aren't traditionally venture-backable.
key_points: Single-LP fund structures allow investors to be more patient and experimental with their investments
Examples like Kapor Capital and Omidyar Network blend social impact goals with financial returns in ways traditional VC can't
Tech wealth has an opportunity to revive philanthropy's original purpose of funding societal risk-taking
Corporate venture arms like Bloomberg Beta can pursue non-strategic investments when freed from traditional LP pressures
The institutionalization of angel investing enables experimentation with non-corporate structures like Bitcoin
money_quotes: With a single LP who's willing to be patient and experiment, investors can be more flexible with what they fund.
Tech wealth has an unprecedented chance to bring us back to the original intent of philanthropy: to provide society with the freedom to take risks.
What do the financial returns look like on models like these? I have no idea. And maybe that's the point.
Omidyar goes as far as to call themselves a 'philanthropic investment firm'.
Unlike a foundation, Breakout Labs only funds for-profit businesses, and terms include a royalty stream and equity.
posts.key_insight: Single-LP venture funds could bridge the gap between traditional VC and philanthropy by freeing investors from IRR pressures to fund patient, experimental, and socially beneficial ventures.

posts.summary: Asparouhova reflects on Y Combinator's Female Founder Conference, praising its deliberate avoidance of typical 'women in tech' topics like sexism and work-life balance. Instead, the conference focused purely on how to build companies, allowing speakers to emerge as multidimensional individuals rather than representatives of their gender. She argues that creating separate sectors for women, minorities, or social impact actually makes people one-dimensional and works against true inclusion. The post advocates for breaking down identity-based silos rather than building new ones.
key_points: The conference's refusal to focus on gendered topics allowed speakers to be seen as individuals with unique perspectives rather than 'women founders'
Building identity-based sectors (women entrepreneurship, social entrepreneurship, etc.) is counterproductive because it flattens people into one-dimensional categories
Exclusion is multidimensional—engineers vs non-engineers, introverts vs extroverts—and can't be solved by simple demographic categories
Not every female founder wants to be known primarily as a female founder; some simply want to be recognized for excellence among all peers
True inclusion comes from breaking down sectors, not creating new siloed ones
money_quotes: Building sectors is dangerous because it makes us one-dimensional.
Not every female founder wants to be known as a female founder. Not every female VC wants to be known as a female VC.
We are awash in sectors that are ostensibly trying to make business better. Social impact has become siloed.
By not affixing the same, flat, 'women X' label onto everyone, the Female Founder Conference allowed everyone in attendance to breathe as themselves.
A straight white male may still feel left out for not being technical, being the quiet one in a room, or being relegated to a corner of the office.
Am I creating new sectors, or helping to break them down?
posts.key_insight: True inclusion comes not from building identity-based sectors that flatten people into categories, but from breaking them down so individuals can be recognized for their unique qualities and accomplishments.

posts.summary: Asparouhova explores the multifaceted value of creativity beyond professional work, arguing that unstructured creative time is essential for self-discovery and identity formation. She distinguishes between creativity in work (proactive problem-solving) and personal creative pursuits (writing, art, novel experiences). The core insight is that creativity serves as a space to experiment with identity without consequences. Most importantly, she argues that regular creative expression builds confidence by proving we can be vulnerable and survive others' judgment, which makes doing difficult but right things easier in all areas of life.
key_points: Creativity in work means proactive problem-solving versus reactive execution
Personal creative time is less about specific activities and more about unstructured space for thoughts to emerge - 'creativity is serendipity'
Creative pursuits help shape identity by letting us probe edges of who we are and try new things without consequences
Regular creative expression builds confidence by proving we can survive judgment while being vulnerable and authentic
When we believe our ideas are valuable, doing the right thing becomes easier even when it's hard
money_quotes: For me, it's less about the specific activity and more about having unstructured time to sit with my thoughts and see what bubbles up. Creativity is serendipity.
It gives us a space to probe at the edges of who we are, try new things without worrying about the consequences, and in doing so, affirm or deny new aspects of our identities.

Putting something into the world 'just because', whether that's a painting or a choreographed act or just wearing something different or doing something silly in public, gives us confidence that we can be our most vulnerable and authentic selves and survive the judgment of others.
It reaffirms that our ideas are valuable. And when we believe our ideas are valuable, that makes 'doing the right thing', even when it's the hard thing, so much easier elsewhere in life.
When we can clearly articulate who we are, and what we believe in, the 'whys' and the 'must-do's' of our livelihoods reveal themselves.
posts.key_insight: Regular creative expression builds the confidence to be vulnerable and authentic, which makes it easier to do the right thing even when it's difficult.

posts.summary: In response to Chris Dixon's concern that proprietary services are winning over open protocols, and Ev Williams' pessimistic comparison to radio and TV's centralization, Asparouhova argues the internet may be different. She identifies four forces pushing toward openness: empowered content creators who can leave platforms, low barriers to building products, the rise of patronage models, and blockchain technology. Her key insight is that unlike traditional media, the internet isn't a single distribution channel but an amalgamation of many, with new ones constantly emerging. This means the innovation-to-centralization cycle repeats rapidly, with empires built and broken overnight.
key_points: Content creators with sufficient audience can always take their followers elsewhere, creating tension with centralized platforms
Patronage models (Patreon, direct fan support) could free creators from platform dependence
The internet fundamentally differs from radio/TV because it's not one distribution channel but many channels constantly being created
Both centralization pessimists and decentralization optimists may be right—the cycle just repeats faster on the internet
money_quotes: In the end, with enough critical mass, mini moguls can always take their audience elsewhere.
The easier it is for anybody to shape the internet, the more agency people will want over their ideas.
Radio or television ARE distribution channels; the internet is an amalgamation of many of those channels - many radios or televisions - and new ones are being created all the time.
Empires are built and broken overnight, and the pendulum will continue to swing back and forth.
If successful, patronage could wean more creators off of centralized platforms.
posts.key_insight: The internet differs from traditional media because it contains multitudes of distribution channels constantly being created, so while each channel may centralize, the rapid cycle of innovation means the pendulum keeps swinging.

posts.summary: Asparouhova argues that what each generation labels as 'laziness' is actually technological progress that frees humans for higher-order thinking. She traces how calculators, GPS, and now on-demand services have all faced accusations of making people lazy, yet each has been normalized over time. Drawing a parallel to programming, where libraries and frameworks reduced repetitive coding, she suggests menial task automation enables more sophisticated problem-solving. She concludes with evidence that IQ gains come specifically from abstract reasoning—the very skills freed up when technology handles routine tasks.
key_points: Each generation accuses new convenience technologies of causing laziness, from calculators to GPS to on-demand apps
Programming evolved from reference books to frameworks precisely because automating repetitive work enables higher-level thinking
The Flynn Effect shows IQ gains come from improvements in abstract reasoning, not memorization—exactly what automation enables
Resistance to convenience technologies eventually fades as their benefits become normalized
Less time on menial tasks means more capacity for innovation and complex problem-solving
money_quotes: Nobody thinks using a GPS or a calculator is a sign of laziness anymore.
Less time spent on menial coding means more time to think smartly about what you're building.
Less time spent on menial tasks means more time for humans to focus on higher cognitive goals.
Our increase in IQ comes not from improvement on concrete or memorization tasks, but on those requiring abstractions and hypotheticals.
New technologies aren't making us lazier. They're making us smarter.
posts.key_insight: What each generation calls 'laziness' is actually the automation of menial tasks that frees human cognition for increasingly abstract and innovative thinking.

posts.summary: Nadia reflects on leaving Collaborative Fund after one year, framing her departure through Milan Kundera's concept of 'Es muss sein' - the idea that our most meaningful choices feel like inescapable fate rather than free will. She joined to work on experimental alternatives to traditional VC but shifted to deploying their seed fund. Despite loving the organization and its values, she couldn't ignore what was 'screaming most loudly' in her head. She expresses gratitude for what venture capital taught her about human nature while signaling a move toward new ways of directing capital to ideas that power the internet.
key_points: Meaningful life choices feel less like free will and more like inescapable imperatives we cannot ignore
She pivoted from her original experimental role to supporting core fund deployment out of loyalty to the organization
Despite perfect alignment on paper, she couldn't suppress what felt like her true calling
Venture capital revealed both 'the weird and wonderful nature of the human condition' and human capacity for kindness
She's moving toward finding new ways to fund valuable internet-related ideas
money_quotes: We do not make them out of free will; our choices are an inescapable fate, an 'imperative enslaving us'.
We can't ignore the things that scream most loudly in our heads, even when our paper lives seem to check all the boxes.
Venture capital showed me the weird and wonderful nature of the human condition.
It also gave me deep faith in the capacity of my fellow humans for kindness and generosity.
Finding new ways to direct capital towards valuable ideas that power the internet.
posts.key_insight: The choices that define us aren't made through rational deliberation but through an inner imperative we eventually cannot resist, even when everything looks right on paper.

posts.summary: Asparouhova reflects on her uncle FM-2030, a transhumanist who cryonically preserved his body believing 2030 would be 'a magical time.' While her family once dismissed his futurism as foolish, she now recognizes that technological change is exponential rather than linear, making the future arrive faster than anyone anticipated. This realization shifts her focus from grand technological achievements to a simpler question: does our daily behavior make society more thoughtful and collaborative?
key_points: Her uncle FM-2030 was a transhumanist who changed his name decades ago because he believed 2030 would be transformative
Technological change is exponential, not linear—we may see 20,000 years of progress in this century
The pace of change from 2010-2015 exceeded that of 1980-1985, making the next 15 years unimaginable
The proximity of dramatic change (including potential human extinction) makes daily behavior feel more consequential
What matters is not working on flashy tech but whether your actions make society more thoughtful and collaborative
money_quotes: Nobody could quantify when 'the future' would happen, but the stuff of sci fi novels was obviously light years away.
The older I get, the more I understand that thousands of years of past data cannot predict the future.
It is not farfetched to suggest that we could see the destruction of humankind within our lifetimes. That is not sci fi. That is real.
Suddenly, how we spend our days feels a lot more important.
Does your day-to-day behavior make our society more thoughtful and collaborative, sustain rather than destruct, unite rather than polarize?
Do you, as an individual, affirm our humanity?
posts.key_insight: Exponential technological change makes our everyday choices about kindness and collaboration more urgent than our contributions to grand technological projects.

posts.summary: The post argues that people cannot be expected to share their authentic selves on broadcast social media because the platforms lack adequate tools for emotional response. When we share online, we seek validation and empathy, but social media's limited response mechanisms (primarily 'like' buttons) only allow positive reactions. This creates discomfort when someone shares negative emotions, as others cannot appropriately respond without commenting. The author suggests that platforms like Buzzfeed's reaction buttons show promise for more nuanced emotional expression, and that expanding beyond binary like/dislike could change what content people feel comfortable sharing.
key_points: Sharing online is fundamentally about seeking validation and empathy from listeners, what John Gottman calls 'bids'
Online communication strips away nonverbal cues, forcing us to develop workarounds like emojis, letter stretching, and gifs
The 'like' button only conveys positive responses, making it impossible to appropriately acknowledge sad or difficult posts
When people cannot respond appropriately to emotional content, they stay silent, which discourages authentic sharing
Platforms could enable more authentic expression by offering a wider range of emotional response options beyond binary like/dislike
money_quotes: While I admire the sentiment, as it stands today, I don't think we can expect people to share their unfiltered selves in a one-to-many format (i.e. broadcast social media).
When we share something about ourselves, online or offline, we're looking for validation from our listeners.
If somebody shares that they are feeling sad on Facebook, it makes people uncomfortable because they are unable to exhibit an appropriate emotional response.
This is as frustrating as though in the physical world, we were unable to do anything but smile.
As a result, most readers simply won't say anything at all, leaving the poster feeling that nobody is listening to them, and making them less likely to post this type of story in the future.
posts.key_insight: The design of social media reaction mechanisms shapes what emotions people feel safe expressing, and platforms that only allow positive responses inadvertently discourage authentic self-expression.

posts.summary: Asparouhova reflects on a period of intentional silence and offline experimentation, during which she tested ideas and revised her beliefs through real-world engagement. She acknowledges the discomfort of uncertainty and the emotional volatility of iterating on new directions. The post centers on a reframing of her identity: rejecting the 'entrepreneur' label in favor of 'artist.' She concludes that the driving force behind her work isn't a clear business plan but rather the conviction that she still has something meaningful to contribute.
key_points: Periods of silence can be productive—listening and testing ideas offline leads to genuine learning and unexpected insights
Uncertainty and emotional volatility are inherent to creative iteration, not signs of failure
The 'entrepreneur' identity feels inadequate for work driven by expression rather than business outcomes
Artistic persistence comes from believing you have something left to say, not from knowing the outcome
money_quotes: I keep doing what I do, even when everybody around me doesn't get it, because I feel like I still have something interesting left to say.
Some of the things I felt most sure about, I've now rejected.
I'm not an entrepreneur. I never liked the label for myself. I'd rather think of myself as an artist.
Sometimes it's not clear what exactly needs to be said, or what form it will take, or even whether it will have any impact on the world.
As long as I feel I have something interesting left to say, I'm going to keep saying it.
posts.key_insight: The motivation to keep creating comes not from certainty about outcomes, but from the conviction that you still have something meaningful left to express.

posts.summary: The post examines crowdfunding's limitations and asks what its next evolution might look like. Sparked by debate over Marco Arment funding his podcast app through patronage, Nadia argues that crowdfunding is essentially a marketing campaign that disadvantages creators without strong personal brands. She contends that crowdfunding validates society's desire to see creativity exist, but the marketing requirements are a bug, not a feature. She points to AngelList Syndicates as a model that aggregates capital while simplifying the process, and asks what an equivalent would look like for creative crowdfunding.
key_points: Crowdfunding is fundamentally a marketing campaign requiring creators to solicit many small checks instead of one large one
Not every skilled creator has or wants to build a strong personal brand, and many interesting ideas are inherently unpopular
Crowdfunding validates society's desire to see creative and 'crazy' things exist in the world
AngelList Syndicates represent an evolution that aggregates crowd capital while presenting a single check to recipients
money_quotes: One of the biggest limitations of crowdfunding is that it is essentially a marketing campaign.
If we acknowledge that creative contributions are valuable to society, the marketing-heavy nature of running a successful Kickstarter campaign starts to look more like a bug than a feature.
Surely we can agree that not every skilled creator has a strong personal brand, nor cares to build one.
Many creators are particularly not disposed to personal brand building, because some of the most interesting ideas are also the most unpopular.
How do we take what crowdfunding has arguably validated - that we want to see crazy things exist in the world - and capture the remaining value?
posts.key_insight: Crowdfunding proves people want to fund creativity, but its marketing-heavy model excludes creators who lack personal brands—the next evolution should separate fundraising ability from creative talent.

posts.summary: Nadia Asparouhova responds to an Atlantic article arguing that programmers shouldn't call themselves engineers. While she agrees that many developers today simply connect prefabricated code, she argues there exists a 'quiet class' of true software engineers who build and maintain digital infrastructure. Using OpenSSL and the Heartbleed bug as her central example, she shows how a tiny team of unpaid volunteers maintained code used by two-thirds of the world's web servers. These infrastructure maintainers exhibit the same pride, responsibility, and pressure as civil engineers, yet remain invisible and unappreciated until something goes wrong.
key_points: Digital infrastructure exists alongside physical infrastructure - the layers of tools, languages, libraries and frameworks that make modern programming easy
OpenSSL, used by two-thirds of web servers, was maintained by 11 volunteers with less than $2,000 in annual donations before Heartbleed
Infrastructure maintainers work under immense pressure knowing their code is used by banks, weapons systems, and governments worldwide
Tech media perpetuates stereotypes of overpaid developers while ignoring those 'toiling thanklessly under the hood'
The ease of modern programming is itself evidence of the invisible engineers who made it possible
money_quotes: Digital infrastructure may not be as permanent as physical infrastructure. It may change frequently. But millions of people still depend on digital infrastructure to function.
These tools are actively maintained by volunteers who work relentlessly, without pay, to ensure a programmer's favorite Python http library doesn't break.

It takes nerves of steel to work for many years on hundreds of thousands of lines of very complex code, with every line of code you touch visible to the world, knowing that code is used by banks, firewalls, weapons systems, web sites, smart phones, industry, government, everywhere.
Knowing that you'll be ignored and unappreciated until something goes wrong.
It's become acceptable in tech media to portray software developers as self-entitled, overpaid neckbeards.

If Bogost is correct, and today's computer programmers don't really do that much complicated work, then we must acknowledge there is still a quiet class of true 'software engineers' who are working hard to make it easy on the rest of us.
They're making it so easy for us, in fact, that we forget they're even there.
posts.key_insight: The very ease of modern software development proves the existence of true software engineers - the invisible infrastructure maintainers whose thankless work makes everyone else's job look simple.

posts.summary: Nadia Asparouhova defends the Chan Zuckerberg Initiative's LLC structure as a natural evolution of philanthropy rather than a cynical tax dodge. She argues that today's philanthropists, shaped by Silicon Valley culture, want measurable returns on social investments rather than traditional charitable giving. The essay traces how this investment-oriented mindset has produced successful models like Kiva and Kickstarter, and suggests VCs are becoming the new foundations. Asparouhova concludes that the LLC structure actually advances ideas that philanthropy reformers have long advocated: flexibility, accountability, and business-like metrics for social change.
key_points: The new generation of philanthropists wants measurable returns on social investment, not guilt-driven charity
An LLC structure offers more flexibility than nonprofits for diverse approaches: grants, loans, investments, and big bets
VCs are becoming the new foundations, funding mission-driven companies in health, food, and urban areas
Silicon Valley views government and philanthropy similarly: as investors in citizens rather than protectors from capitalism
The Chan Zuckerberg Initiative actually implements reforms that traditional philanthropy advocates have long pushed for
money_quotes: This generation of philanthropists, big or small, wants to see a return on their social investment.
They despise financial leeches, charities begging for money in, money out, and are excited by models that help other people help themselves.
If philanthropy is an investment portfolio, an LLC allows dollars to go towards grants, loans, crazy big bets, and everything in between.
Tech startup founders see the government as an investor in citizens, rather than as a protector from capitalism.
In this new era of philanthropy, one could argue that VCs are the new foundations.
Let's call the Chan Zuckerberg Initiative what it is: a radical, albeit expensive, experiment in philanthropy.
posts.key_insight: Silicon Valley's investment-oriented approach to philanthropy—demanding accountability, measurable outcomes, and flexible vehicles like LLCs—represents a natural evolution that traditional philanthropy reformers have long advocated for, not a rejection of charitable giving.

posts.summary: Asparouhova examines the puzzling Pew Research finding that 40% of millennials support government censorship of speech offensive to minorities, despite being the most tolerant generation on social issues. She argues this apparent paradox stems from how social media has transformed speech itself—we share outrage-inducing content to strengthen our identities, and collective online shaming has become a form of peer-to-peer censorship. The essay suggests that while individually exercising free speech, we collectively create censorious mob dynamics that ruin lives. She concludes that we've achieved a dystopian version of world peace: not diverse tolerance, but citizens policing adherence to politically correct views.
key_points: 40% of millennials supporting government censorship seems paradoxical given their high tolerance for diversity and progressive social views
Increased access to information online has counterintuitively made people want to censor more, not less
Social media outrage-sharing strengthens personal identity but contributes to collective censorship through mob dynamics
Censorship is no longer primarily a government function—it happens through social media pile-ons and public shaming
Edge cases where speech is most offensive are precisely where free speech protections matter most
money_quotes: It seems counterintuitive that increased access to information online should make us want to censor more, not less.
These are the edge cases where the protection of free speech matters most.
The content that pisses us off the most is also the content we share the most, because it strengthens our identity.
We all face a choice: keep sharing that content and getting mad about it, or recognize the futile masturbatory nature of our acts and let it go.
These days, with enough angry retweets, anyone can censor anything.
Writing an angry post about so-and-so's latest offensive remark is technically an exercise of your right to free speech, but as part of a critical mass, also contributes to censorship of your peers.

World peace is here, but it's a little dystopian. Rather than the colorful patchwork quilt envisioned by 1960s free-love hippies, we've got citizens policing adherence to a politically correct set of views.
posts.key_insight: Social media has democratized censorship—individual acts of free speech aggregate into collective mob dynamics that suppress dissent more effectively than government ever could.

posts.summary: In the wake of Apple's refusal to help the FBI unlock a San Bernardino shooter's iPhone, Asparouhova draws a parallel to India's ban on Facebook's Free Basics. She argues that people's positions on these issues reveal whether they fundamentally trust corporations or governments with authority. The core insight is that both sectors deserve equal scrutiny since both are run by fallible humans. She warns against replacing government as 'benevolent dictator' with tech companies, advocating instead for checks and balances that apply universally.
key_points: Support for Apple's backdoor resistance combined with support for Free Basics reveals inconsistent trust in corporate vs. government power
Both private and public sectors are run by humans equally subject to error, cultural norms, and temptation
Historical trust in government as neutral arbiter was always misplaced, as evidenced by constitutional compromises like the Three-Fifths Compromise
As technology reshapes governance, we risk simply swapping one benevolent dictator (government) for another (tech companies)
The goal should be creating checks and balances that apply to all concentrations of power, not selective skepticism
money_quotes: But humans are humans, whether they work for the government or for a large tech company.
To support Free Basics but oppose backdoors would be to commit the same fallacy as our predecessors. We are still putting faith in a benevolent dictator (companies) instead of in ourselves.
The private and public sector deserve equal scrutiny.
Governments are made of people, and people are subject to the same cultural norms, influences and temptations as the rest of us.
As we reimagine government in a world driven by technology, let's not accidentally replace one benevolent dictator with another.
Our goal should be to create a system of checks and balances that apply to everyone.
posts.key_insight: Selective distrust of either government or corporations while trusting the other ignores that both are human institutions equally prone to abuse of power.

posts.summary: Asparouhova responds to the expulsion of Larry Garfield from the Drupal community over his private BDSM lifestyle, arguing that while the justification has shifted from 'family values' to 'gender equality,' the persecution of people for their beliefs remains equally problematic. She criticizes the political climate that pressures leaders like Dries Buytaert to make such decisions, and holds the broader community—including herself—responsible for not defending others against belief-based persecution. The piece warns that demands for diversity and equality are paradoxically creating a new conformity where private beliefs become grounds for public expulsion.
key_points: The justification for persecution has evolved from 'threat to family values' to 'threat to gender equality,' but the outcome—punishing people for beliefs—remains the same
Beliefs are distinct from actions, and persecuting people for what they believe undermines free and open democracy
The community that values diversity created the conditions for this persecution by not standing up when others were unfairly targeted
What presents itself as equality is increasingly a form of coercion that demands conformity rather than genuine diversity
money_quotes: In the past, Dries might've kicked Larry out because 'BDSM is a threat to family values'. Today, leaders like Dries kick Larry out because 'BDSM is a threat to gender equality'. Unfortunately, the end result is the same.
Beliefs are not actions. We cannot persecute people for what they believe, no matter how much it disgusts us, and simultaneously maintain a free and open democracy.
We are all responsible for the increasingly alarming practice of coercion masquerading as equality.
If you're upset about Dries's decision, just remember: you created this.
If diversity is our dogma, call me 'spiritual, not religious'. I still pray for the same things as you, but I won't be at the witch trials.
Diversity is starting to look a lot more like conformity.
posts.key_insight: When communities police private beliefs in the name of progressive values, they replicate the same persecution they claim to oppose—just with updated justifications.

posts.summary: Asparouhova reflects on her complicated relationship with silence around experiences of harassment and abuse of power in tech and other industries. She examines why she, like many women, has kept quiet—normalizing trauma, fearing self-blame, and wanting to define herself by her work rather than victimization. Reading Ronan Farrow's nuanced New Yorker piece on Weinstein finally resonated because it captured the messy psychological reality rather than a simple good-vs-evil narrative. She concludes that true power will come not from public confession or continued silence, but from emotional indifference—no longer flinching at perpetrators' names or avoiding spaces where they might be.
key_points: Binary media portrayals of harassment flatten complex experiences into good-vs-evil narratives that feel reductive and fuel unhelpful public outrage cycles
Victims often normalize trauma by maintaining relationships with perpetrators, blaming themselves, and telling themselves it wasn't that bad
Silence feels protective but actually bonds victims more tightly to their abusers—it's an active choice that requires constant maintenance
Women report as groups not just for stronger evidence but because it keeps the spotlight on perpetrators rather than putting victims' identities on the chopping block
True recovery means reaching emotional indifference—not caring anymore when you see their name or face
money_quotes: When bad things happen to you, you do all the things that these women described in the New Yorker piece. You tell yourself it wasn't so bad, or maybe it was your fault, or you're blowing things out of proportion.
I used to think that not feeling black-and-white about my experiences meant that it must have been my fault, too.

That fuzziness is not because it's unclear whether 'something really did happen' or not, but rather an expected reaction to trauma, in which you try to normalize the behavior and empathize with the other person in order to make sense of the very nonsensical, out-of-control things that have happened to you.
I want to believe that by keeping silent, I'm making the stories go away and living my life as I want. But silence is an active choice, too.
Being this quiet, and working this hard to make sure the stories don't get out, means I am only bonding myself even more irrevocably to these people. Love and hate are equal and opposing forces.
I'd rather define myself by what I do than what's been done to me.
This isn't a story about sex, assault, or even harassment. It's the story that accompanies every abuse of power.
posts.key_insight: Silence around trauma feels like self-protection but actually maintains the bond to perpetrators; true freedom comes not from public confession or continued secrecy, but from reaching genuine emotional indifference.

posts.summary: Asparouhova critiques voting as a governance mechanism, arguing it was designed for 18th-century conditions that no longer apply. She identifies three core problems: voting can never be truly representative (especially in decentralized communities where total population is unknown), it promotes competition over cooperation, and it creates a veneer of objectivity that absolves people of genuine compromise. She proposes cooperative governance as an alternative, drawing inspiration from cooperative board games and modern open source communities that use lazy consensus and consensus-seeking processes. The key insight is that cooperative mechanics flip incentives—making objections expensive rather than cheap—which could better suit our attention-scarce, reputation-driven era.
key_points: Voting was invented for small, homogenous populations (~1 million eligible voters in 1800 America) and doesn't scale to global, decentralized communities where total population is unknown
Voting is a zero-sum competitive game that promotes faction formation and conflict rather than cooperation, with no checks on well-coordinated factions spanning branches of government
Modern open source communities like Node.js and Rust have pioneered 'lazy consensus' and 'consensus seeking' as alternatives where voting is considered the failure outcome
Cooperative games work because they make vetoing expensive (requiring reputation to raise objections) while voting makes disagreement cheap, leading to performative outrage
Reputation is becoming more powerful than money as a form of influence, and cooperative governance leverages this by letting attention and expertise determine influence
money_quotes: If we're coming up with wild ideas for social coordination, voting seems like an awfully familiar solution.

Over-engineered systems create a 'veneer of objectivity' that lets us put blind faith into something greater than ourselves—and in doing so, absolve ourselves of the very real, hard work of disagreeing and compromising with our fellow humans.
If humans are good at one thing, it is circumvention.
It seems paradoxical to call decentralization 'revolutionary' while also preaching government by automation.
Voting is a last resort. A healthy community should rarely need to vote.
Under voting, it's cheap to veto, which has led to an anti-pattern of outrage today. Not only is it cheap to disagree, but you're often rewarded for it.
Votes can be manipulated by propaganda and special interests. Reputation takes time to build, but it belongs to you.
Voting wasn't designed for scale, and it's not going to carry us through the next era of governance.
posts.key_insight: Cooperative governance mechanisms that make objections expensive and consensus the default state are better suited to our modern era of unknown populations, scarce attention, and reputation-based influence than the competitive, zero-sum voting systems designed for 18th-century conditions.

posts.summary: Nadia explores why software developers struggle to monetize their own code despite their skills being highly valued in the job market. She traces three eras of developer monetization: selling code (1980s-90s, largely failed due to zero marginal cost and developers creating alternatives), selling time (late 1990s-present, works but doesn't let developers profit from their own projects), and selling access/reputation (emerging). The piece argues that as code becomes increasingly commoditized and automatable, a developer's unique reputation and personal brand may become more valuable than the code itself. She suggests developers could charge for prioritization, knowledge sharing, or brand association rather than trying to sell code directly.
key_points: Developers' code-writing skills have market value, but their output (the code itself) often has none due to zero marginal cost and easy replication
Selling to developers is like 'selling ice to an Eskimo' because they can build alternatives themselves, especially for fun problems
Employment funds developers' lifestyles but forces them to write employer-chosen code rather than their own projects
As code becomes more interchangeable and automatable, fewer developers may be needed to perform the same tasks over time
A developer's reputation has fewer substitutes than their code, making it a potentially more sustainable funding mechanism
money_quotes: When a developer produces code of their own choosing, they quickly find that while their code-writing skills have market value, their output (the code they write) has none.
Selling to developers is a bit like selling ice to an Eskimo.
If code is your output, and code is basically knowledge, then over time, we don't need to write as much code as we used to.

So long as we focus on code, we'll keep coming back to the problem of substitutes. Code has many substitutes. Getting paid to write code has many substitutes (whether human or machine). But a developer's reputation has fewer substitutes.
The definition of open source says nothing about how it gets produced, only how it is distributed.
In the early 1990s, it seemed inconceivable that people wouldn't pay for software. Today, it might seem inconceivable that developers might eventually not sell their time.
posts.key_insight: As code becomes increasingly commoditized and automatable, developers' sustainable path to capturing value may shift from selling code or time to monetizing their irreplaceable personal reputation and brand.

posts.summary: Asparouhova applies Hirschman's economic theory of 'exit vs voice' to explain why online outrage has exploded: users can't leave dominant platforms due to network effects and data lock-in, so they resort to voice (outrage) instead. She argues that while regulation and platform governance reforms represent 'voice,' they paradoxically strengthen our ties to these platforms. The piece proposes two alternatives: off-platform exits (data portability, new platforms) and on-platform exits (private messengers, blocking tools, groups, Stories). She suggests that embracing 'filter bubbles' and letting communities shard into smaller groups might be healthier than forcing everyone into the same digital space, while acknowledging this could create 'knowledge ghettos' where influencers retreat from public discourse.
key_points: Platform lock-in transforms dissent into outrage—when users can't leave, they resort to voice, which manifests as the anger we see online
Regulation and platform governance reforms, while well-intentioned, actually strengthen our dependency on existing platforms rather than enabling alternatives
True platform sovereignty lies with the network (users), not the platforms themselves—Twitter and Facebook have no inherent value except as agreed-upon gathering places
On-platform exits (private messengers, blocking tools, groups, Stories) offer a more realistic path than building new platforms, essentially letting people 'shard' into smaller communities
Filter bubbles might actually be healthy—designing for smaller, context-rich communities rather than forcing everyone into the same space
money_quotes: Maybe outrage is dissent without an outlet, a byproduct of platform lock-in with nowhere to hide and nowhere else to go.
When people can't exit, they resort to voice, which looks a lot like outrage.
Twitter has no inherent value, except that it's where we agree to gather, find, and talk to each other.
By investing in voice, we're doubling down on a bad relationship instead of exploring alternatives.
Facebook isn't a community. It's a landowner: the wide plains and open skies to which communities flock.
If platforms treat 'exit' as a feature rather than a bug, they can retain their users by offering spaces for smaller, context-rich interactions.
Given our scale today, maybe bubbles aren't such a bad thing. Maybe they're just realistic.
Platforms have to choose whether to provide exit or voice, but as the de facto governments of the digital world, they also can't sit on the fence and do neither. To do nothing is autocracy.
posts.key_insight: Online outrage is fundamentally an economic problem of platform lock-in—when users can't exit, voice becomes rage, and the solution may be designing for smaller communities rather than forcing universal discourse.

posts.summary: Nadia announces she's leaving GitHub to join Protocol Labs as a researcher focused on developing new economic models for software production. She reflects on the broader challenge of understanding incentives in digital infrastructure, noting that current research doesn't adequately address modern challenges. She'll also be a visiting Fellow at Harvard Kennedy School. The post frames open source software as a valuable lens for understanding behavioral patterns because of its thorough documentation.
key_points: Current research doesn't adequately explain how to structure incentives for digital infrastructure, platforms, and online content
Open source software's thorough documentation makes it ideal for studying behavioral patterns around production
Paid research opportunities exploring these questions are extremely rare in tech
Protocol Labs represents a rare organization willing to tackle hard problems with long timelines
money_quotes: We know shockingly little about how to structure incentives in our modern world, and current research doesn't have much to say about today's challenges.
Open source software, because it's so thoroughly documented, is a great way to understand these behavioral patterns.
It turns out that thinking about these problems on my nights and weekends is a recipe for creative exhaustion.
There are few companies who not only share my interest in solving hard problems with long timelines, but who structure their organization and funding model accordingly.
posts.key_insight: Open source software serves as an ideal laboratory for understanding the broader challenge of structuring incentives for digital goods production, but finding institutional support for this research is exceptionally rare.

posts.summary: This post introduces Elinor Ostrom's Nobel Prize-winning work on common pool resource management as a counterpoint to pessimistic game theory models like the tragedy of the commons. Ostrom demonstrated that communities can sustainably manage shared resources without market or state intervention when certain conditions are met: defined boundaries, localized rules, monitoring, and high mutual trust among participants. The author connects this framework to open source sustainability, arguing that the production of digital public goods has rivalrous properties similar to physical commons. Rather than seeking a single solution to open source funding, Ostrom's work suggests cataloging behavioral differences between communities to find emergent patterns. The post concludes that high-context, self-organized solutions are more likely to succeed than low-context external interventions.
key_points: Ostrom's research shows commons can be sustainably managed without market or state intervention when communities have defined boundaries, localized rules, and accountability mechanisms
Sustainable commons management requires high context among participants - most failures trace back to context collapse
Communities need high mutual trust and long-term interest (low discount rate) to self-regulate effectively
Scaling doesn't mean cramming more people into one community, but acknowledging boundaries and governing at multiple levels (polycentric approach)
High-context solutions like funding specific contributors are more likely to succeed than low-context solutions like bounties to strangers
money_quotes: Sustainably managing the commons requires a high degree of context among participants. Most failure outcomes can be traced back to context collapse.
Managing at scale doesn't mean stuffing more and more people into the same community, but acknowledging boundaries and working together to govern at multiple levels.
Nobody wants to be told the answer is complicated, whereas the other models are simple, even if they're pessimistic and reductive.
Perhaps the goal is to understand and catalog these behavioral differences, so that commonalities emerge between them.

Maybe now is the time to cast aside the market and the state as our saviors, and instead consider an alternative: one that recognizes the ability of people to organize and design solutions for themselves.
When individuals have developed shared norms and patterns of reciprocity, they possess social capital with which they can build institutional arrangements for resolving CPR dilemmas.
posts.key_insight: Communities can sustainably manage shared resources through high-context self-organization rather than external intervention, but this requires defined boundaries, mutual trust, and long-term commitment from participants.

posts.summary: Asparouhova draws parallels between app developers and open source developers, arguing both are 'toolmakers' facing similar challenges: low revenue despite high adoption, entitled users, and dependence on platforms (Apple's App Store and GitHub) that don't serve their interests. She examines how platform policies constrain monetization options, leading to poor incentives where 'scammy' developers thrive while those who 'respect humanity' struggle.
